title: The Future of American Economic Power
gist: Peter Harrell (Carnegie Endowment) maps U.S. economic power across six sources - productive, market, financial, chokepoint, platform, and rule-setting - and argues Trump's aggressive economic statecraft (trade deals with sweeping geopolitical riders, chip-export diplomacy, critical-minerals equity dealmaking, and a stablecoin strategy) is entrenching some forms of U.S. dominance while triggering an allied and adversarial backlash that is eroding others, with AI's own trajectory as the largest swing factor for whether U.S. hegemony holds over the next two decades.

insights.claim: Peter Harrell frames U.S. economic power as six distinct, overlapping sources: productive power (capacity to make goods/services), market power (scale of the U.S. market), financial power/dollar dominance, chokepoint power (control of critical nodes like semiconductors), platform and corporate power (leverage via U.S.-controlled tech platforms), and rule-setting power (ability to set global economic norms).
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Gives a taxonomy for scoring which specific levers of U.S. power are strengthening vs. eroding, rather than treating 'American economic power' as a single monolithic trend.
insights.quote: There are six interrelated types of economic power: productive power, market power, financial power, chokepoint power, platform and corporate power, and the government's power or capacity to act as a global rule-setter.
insights.locator: Six Sources of American Economic Power

insights.claim: Chokepoint power can be wielded by economically small states: Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz (through which ~20% of world oil flows) gives Tehran major leverage despite a 2025 GDP smaller than the Denver, Colorado metro area, while China demonstrated the same dynamic in spring 2025 by cutting off rare-earths exports essential to industrial and defense supply chains.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Chokepoint leverage is decoupled from GDP size - small or mid-size states/actors controlling a single critical node (a strait, a mineral) can impose outsized costs, a risk model applicable beyond Iran and China.
insights.quote: a bit smaller than the GDP of the Denver, Colorado, metro area
insights.locator: Chokepoint power:

insights.claim: U.S. sanctions against the International Criminal Court resulted in the court's employees losing access to corporate email, Uber, and Amazon services, illustrating how the U.S. government can direct American platform companies to withdraw services from an institution as a coercive tool.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Concrete precedent for how far U.S. platform power can reach into a nominally allied institution's daily operations - relevant to any entity assessing exposure to U.S. platform dependence.
insights.quote: Recent U.S. sanctions against the International Criminal Court, for instance, resulted in the court's employees losing access to corporate email functions, Uber, and Amazon, among other services.
insights.locator: Platform and corporate power:

themes.theme: Weaponized interdependence and minimum viable scale

insights.claim: Economist Albert Hirschman's 1945 trade-dependence theory holds that a country whose trade is concentrated with a larger, less-dependent partner sits in a structurally weaker power position - explaining why Washington has far more leverage over Mexico (which sends 80% of its goods exports to the U.S.) than over India (under 20%).
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Predicts which trading partners are structurally more coercible by U.S. tariff threats - Mexico is far more exposed than India, a distinction relevant to trade-war positioning.
insights.locator: In his foundational 1945 work, economist Albert Hirschman

insights.claim: Henry Farrell and Abraham Newman's 'weaponized interdependence' thesis holds that the topology of global economic networks - which country controls the key nodes - generates durable, exploitable power imbalances among states, a dynamic the U.S. has used to advance its own security and foreign-policy interests.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Theoretical basis for why U.S. financial/tech chokepoints translate into geopolitical leverage even in transactions the U.S. is not a direct party to.
insights.quote: the “topography” of economic networks—what country controls key nodes—“generates enduring power imbalances among states”
insights.locator: Henry Farrell and Abraham Newman have shown

insights.claim: Harrell's own 'minimum viable scale' corollary argues U.S. power depends less on its aggregate share of a resource (e.g., the dollar's share of global reserves) than on whether rivals can build an alternative network large enough to be usable - once a substitute reaches that threshold, Washington's ability to weaponize interdependence collapses even if its aggregate share stays high.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: The single most actionable framework in the piece: watch minimum-viable-scale thresholds (CIPS, UPI, Mir adoption volumes) rather than aggregate dollar share as the real signal of de-dollarization risk.
insights.quote: Power depends less on America's aggregate share of a type of economic power than it does on whether other countries are able to develop an alternative network with a minimum viable scale to escape American economic hegemony.
insights.locator: This is a “minimum viable scale” corollary

insights.claim: The U.S. sanctions list grew from 912 individuals, companies, and entities in 2000 to more than 17,000 by the end of 2025 - a roughly 19x increase - while the number of distinct U.S. sanctions programs (37) now exceeds the number of countries (20) with which Washington has ratified free trade agreements.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Quantifies the shift from positive to coercive economic statecraft - sanctions exposure is now structurally a bigger part of engaging with U.S. economic policy than trade-agreement access.
insights.quote: In 2000, there were 912 total individuals, companies, and entities on U.S. sanctions lists; at the end of 2025, there were more than 17,000.
insights.locator: This is clear from the numbers.

insights.claim: The Commerce Department's Entity List (an export-restriction tool) held roughly 200 entities in its first decade (1997-2007), grew to 832 additions during 2010-2020, and has added 1,364 more since 2021 alone - an accelerating pace of export-control designations.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Signals accelerating export-control risk for companies operating in China-adjacent supply chains, particularly semiconductors and advanced tech.
insights.locator: The Commerce Department's use of the Entity List

insights.claim: After 9/11, U.S. international economic policy pivoted from system-building (Bretton Woods institutions, WTO, trade deals) to weaponizing existing interdependence via sanctions and export controls, while positive-power efforts stalled: the Doha Round collapsed by 2008, and the major Trans-Pacific Partnership and Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership negotiations failed amid domestic political opposition.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Explains why rule-setting power has stalled for a generation - roughly 25 years without a new major U.S.-led multilateral trade architecture is a structural headwind against renewed U.S.-centered globalization.
insights.locator: After the 9/11 terrorist attacks in 2001

themes.theme: Erosion signals in dollar and sanctions power

insights.claim: By aggregate measures the dollar's global position remains strong: the dollar's share of global reserves has declined only modestly since a 2016 peak and remains above 55%, and the U.S. still holds about 40% of world equity and debt markets.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Aggregate dollar metrics still look strong, cautioning against overreading de-dollarization headlines as an imminent collapse.
insights.locator: Financial power (“dollar dominance”):

insights.claim: Despite intact aggregate dollar share, minimum-viable-scale alternatives have emerged since 2014 (Russia's Mir, China's Cross-Border Interbank Payment System, India's Unified Payments Interface, plus a nascent EU network), and U.S. sanctions have visibly lost bite: pre-2015 Iran sanctions cut exports by over 50% (2011-2014) and ~75% (2017-2020), but 2025 energy-sector sanctions barely dented Iranian crude exports, which rose year-over-year despite renewed 'maximum pressure.'
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: The real signal for investors positioning around Iran/Russia sanctions risk is diminishing marginal deterrence as non-dollar payment rails scale, not the headline sanctions themselves.
insights.quote: Iranian crude oil exports rose in 2025 compared to 2024, despite Trump's promise to again renew maximum pressure sanctions
insights.locator: The impact of these developments is apparent

insights.claim: Russia-China bilateral trade roughly doubled over the last five years despite Western sanctions, and the value of Chinese cross-border payments and receipts made in U.S. dollars began falling in 2022-2023 as both countries increasingly invoice trade outside the dollar.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Confirms a durable, not cyclical, non-dollar trade corridor has formed between the two largest U.S. adversaries - a structural erosion of dollar transactional reach in that relationship.
insights.locator: Sanctions also appear to have had, at most, a limited impact

insights.claim: Nvidia alone accounts for more than 80% of the global market for AI training chips, and three U.S.-based firms control roughly 75% of the global market for semiconductor design software - together giving Washington its strongest remaining chokepoint over China's AI development.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: This is the highest-leverage U.S. chokepoint today; erosion of Nvidia's share or of EDA-software control would be the key signal that this power source is weakening.
insights.quote: with NVIDIA alone accounting for more than 80 percent of the global market for AI training chips last year
insights.locator: Chokepoint power: While the United States

insights.claim: The U.S. share of global semiconductor manufacturing fell from over 30% to roughly 10% by 2022 (prompting the 2022 CHIPS and Science Act), even as the U.S. retains about 70% of global cloud services and overwhelming dominance in smartphone (Android/iOS) and desktop operating systems.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: U.S. productive power in chip fabrication has eroded even as design/software/platform power stays dominant - the durable U.S. edge is shifting upstream (design, cloud, platforms) rather than resting on fabrication.
insights.locator: Productive power: Manufacturing's share

insights.claim: The U.S. share of global GDP has risen to 25-26% (up from just over 20% in 2011) on relative outperformance versus other industrial economies and China's weaker growth, and U.S. goods-and-services imports exceeded $4 trillion in 2025, up roughly 50% over the decade.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Market power - not productive power - is the U.S.'s strongest and still-growing lever, underpinning Trump's tariff-driven trade-deal strategy.
insights.locator: Market power: The United States remains

insights.claim: U.S. market power is diverging by counterparty: it is rising with allies (EU exports to the U.S. up from ~17.5% to ~21.5% of total goods exports over a decade; South Korea's U.S.-bound export share up from 13% to 17%) while falling sharply with China (Chinese exports to the U.S. down from 20% to under 10% of China's total) - explaining why many allies signed Trump-era trade deals while China retaliated instead.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Predicts continued asymmetric negotiating success: expect further deal-making progress with allies and continued stalemate with China as the underlying trade-dependence gap widens.
insights.quote: the United States increasingly has greater market power over many of its allies than it does over Beijing
insights.locator: These data may explain why many U.S. allies

insights.claim: Trump's 'agreements on reciprocal trade' (nearly 20 countries coerced into deals, nine finalized as of the article) go well beyond tariffs: partners such as Argentina and Taiwan have agreed to raise tariffs against China on U.S. request, enforce U.S. sanctions and export controls domestically, give U.S. investors preferential access to mines and critical infrastructure, accept U.S.-standard cars and pharmaceuticals without extra approval, and ban Chinese telecom equipment from network upgrades.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: A durable template for extending U.S. economic-security policy into third countries' domestic law - worth tracking which additional countries sign similarly sweeping terms.
insights.quote: Trade partners such as Argentina and Taiwan have agreed to sweeping commitments to raise tariffs and nontariff barriers against China if asked to do so by the United States
insights.locator: Take trade, for example

insights.claim: The White House frames its chip-export diplomacy as designed to 'ensure that American AI technologies, standards, and governance models are adopted worldwide,' reversing the Biden-era approach of broadly restricting chip exports in favor of actively promoting U.S. chip deployment abroad (except to China) to lock in foreign dependency on U.S. AI infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Bullish signal for U.S. AI/semiconductor exporters as government policy now actively subsidizes their global market share rather than restricting it.
insights.quote: ensure that American AI technologies, standards, and governance models are adopted worldwide to strengthen relationships with our allies and secure our continued technological dominance.
insights.locator: Where the Biden administration aimed to restrict

insights.claim: Trump officials view the administration's removal of the Venezuelan government as further entrenching U.S. power in global energy markets, part of a broader 'energy dominance' agenda intended to build other countries' dependence on U.S. energy exports and civilian nuclear technology.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Geopolitical energy actions are internally read as instruments of economic statecraft, not just security policy - relevant to energy-sector geopolitical risk modeling.
insights.locator: Many Trump officials see his decapitation of the Venezuelan government

insights.claim: The administration is using equity stakes and dealmaking to secure critical minerals: after taking an equity stake in USA Rare Earth, it helped the company acquire a strategic mining asset in Brazil in April 2026, and separately has reportedly threatened to cut HIV treatment funding to Zambia unless it grants the U.S. access to its copper, lithium, and cobalt deposits.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Signals an aggressive resource-linked dealmaking playbook - a green light for equity-stake-style critical-minerals investing but also a diplomatic and reputational risk factor.
insights.quote: reportedly threatened to cut funding for HIV treatment programs in Zambia if the Zambian government fails to accede to U.S. demands for access to the country's copper, lithium, and cobalt deposits.
insights.locator: His administration has even reportedly threatened

insights.claim: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent frames U.S. crypto policy, including dollar-linked stablecoins, explicitly as a tool to reinforce the dollar's global reserve-currency status - the strategic bet is that keeping the leading fintech/stablecoin firms U.S.-domiciled preserves dollar-centric rules even as global finance migrates away from traditional banks.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Stablecoin policy is being used as a dollar-dominance defense mechanism, not just a crypto-industry favor - structurally bullish for U.S.-domiciled stablecoin issuers.
insights.quote: reinforc[e] the U.S. dollar's status as the global reserve currency
insights.locator: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has written

insights.claim: Most of Trump's trade deals remain unimplemented and are unlikely to be sent to Congress for ratification, leaving them vulnerable to being discarded by future administrations or foreign governments - a repeat of first-term Trump manufacturing pledges that firms later abandoned, such as Foxconn's largely scrapped $10 billion Wisconsin project.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Durability risk for any investment thesis premised on these deals holding - treat them as reversible executive-level commitments rather than binding treaties.
insights.quote: most of his trade deals, for example, have not yet been implemented by American trading partners, and Trump does not appear to be planning to ask Congress to ratify them.
insights.locator: Nevertheless, for all the Trump administration's commitment

insights.claim: Trump's conduct has triggered backlash beyond traditional adversaries: the European Union and the United Kingdom - not just Russia and China - are now discussing payment-network alternatives to Visa/Mastercard, and 'digital sovereignty' initiatives are spreading from the EU to India and Brazil, explicitly aimed at reducing dependence on U.S. tech platforms.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: Allied-country de-risking from U.S. financial/tech infrastructure is a structurally significant new trend - bearish long-term for U.S. platform and financial power even among treaty allies.
insights.quote: It is no longer just Russia, China, and other U.S. adversaries and competitors that are discussing building payment networks insulated from U.S. economic coercion; it is also the European Union and the United Kingdom.
insights.locator: It is no longer just Russia, China

insights.claim: Trump's aggressive trade posture has had the paradoxical effect of accelerating EU trade diversification, pushing Brussels out of years of negotiating lethargy to finally conclude sweeping trade agreements with Mercosur, India, Indonesia, Australia, and New Zealand.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: A second-order effect worth monitoring: U.S. pressure is catalyzing non-U.S. trade architecture that could, over time, reduce American market-power leverage over Europe.
insights.locator: Trump's aggressive approach to global trade has spurred

insights.claim: Harrell frames AI's economic trajectory as the largest single wildcard for future U.S. economic power: if U.S. AI leadership drives sustained high growth and deepens foreign dependence on U.S. models, American economic power will almost certainly rise regardless of foreign policy choices, whereas if AI instead causes mass U.S. unemployment, U.S. power will likely wane as the government struggles to manage the fallout.
insights.speaker: Peter Harrell
insights.so_what: This is the master variable to monitor - AI labor-market outcomes could swamp every other economic-statecraft lever discussed in the piece, for better or worse.
insights.quote: If that comes to pass, U.S. global economic power will almost certainly rise irrespective of foreign government policy choices.
insights.locator: Looking to the future, U.S. and global economies
